sc_sutta: 3. Mortifying “These two things, mendicants, are mortifying. What two? It’s when someone has done bad things and not done good things, by way of body, speech, and mind. Thinking, ‘I’ve done bad things by way of body, speech, and mind’, they’re mortified. Thinking, ‘I haven’t done good things by way of body, speech, and mind’, they’re mortified. These are the two things that are mortifying.”
sutta: Monks there are these two things that burn what do herein a certain one has done immoral acts of body he has done immoral acts in speech and thought he has omitted moral acts in body speech and thought he is burned or seared with remorse at the thought i have done wrong in body speech and thought i have left undone the good deeds in body speech and thought and he burns at the thought of it these monks are the two things that burn
commentary: so the buddha says if we have done immoral acts immoral karma in body speech and mind or we have omitted to do moral acts moral karma wholesome karma in body speech and thought then our conscience is pregnant so you see and sometimes it's not only that we do something immoral you know even if we fail to do something that we ought to do later it would burn our conscience and we get pricked by it that's why we have to be careful what are the immoral acts for example uh immoral act of body might be killing committing adultery or stealing and immoral acts of a speech might be lying slandering carrying tales so that people quarrel et cetera and then uh immoral act of thought that might be having a hatred for somebody etc now the next two times
